passages.explanation: Early on Sunday morning, while it's still dark, Mary Magdalene goes to the tomb and finds the stone has been removed. She doesn't go in — she runs to Peter and the beloved disciple and tells them someone has taken Jesus' body. Her assumption is grave robbery, not resurrection.

Peter and the other disciple race to the tomb. The other disciple arrives first, looks in, and sees the linen cloths lying there but doesn't enter. Peter arrives, goes straight in, and sees the burial cloths — including the face cloth, which is folded neatly and set apart from the rest. The other disciple enters, sees the same thing, and believes. John notes that they didn't yet understand from Scripture that Jesus had to rise from the dead — the belief came from what they saw in the tomb. They go home.

Mary stays at the tomb, weeping. She looks inside and sees two angels sitting where Jesus' body had been — one at the head, one at the feet. They ask why she's crying. She says someone has taken her Lord. She turns around and sees Jesus but doesn't recognise him. He asks the same questions: why is she crying, who is she looking for? She assumes he's the gardener and asks if he moved the body. Jesus says one word — 'Mary' — and she immediately recognises him, calling him 'Rabboni' (Teacher). Jesus tells her not to hold on to him because he hasn't yet ascended to the Father. He sends her to tell the disciples: 'I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God.' Mary goes and delivers the message. She's the first person to see the risen Jesus and the first sent to announce it.

That evening, the disciples are gathered behind locked doors because they're afraid of the Jewish authorities. Jesus appears among them — despite the locked doors — and says 'Peace be to you.' He shows them his hands and side. They're overjoyed. He commissions them: as the Father sent him, he's sending them. He breathes on them and says 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' He gives them authority regarding the forgiveness of sins — if they forgive someone's sins, they're forgiven; if they retain them, they're retained.

Thomas wasn't there. When the others tell him they've seen the Lord, he refuses to believe unless he can see and touch the nail marks and the wound in Jesus' side. Eight days later, they're gathered again with the doors locked. Jesus appears the same way and says peace to them. He goes directly to Thomas: reach out your finger, see my hands, put your hand in my side. Stop doubting and believe. Thomas responds with the highest declaration of faith in the entire gospel: 'My Lord and my God.' Jesus says Thomas believed because he saw — but blessed are those who believe without seeing. This statement reaches through the text to every future reader.

John acknowledges that Jesus performed many other signs that aren't recorded in this book. The ones he included were chosen with a specific purpose: so that the reader will believe Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and through believing will have life in his name. This reads as the original ending of the gospel — chapter 21 appears to be an epilogue added afterward.
